summary: DHH describes the planning process for HEY's next development cycle, arguing that the team must deliberately prioritize weird, different, and fun features alongside practical ones. He acknowledges they're late on promised features like custom domains for personal accounts. But he pushes back against users who only want their wishlists fulfilled, arguing that nobody asks for weird or delightful — so it's the team's duty to champion it. The post is a short manifesto for product development that resists being driven purely by feature requests.
key_points: HEY's planning process includes an explicit directive to 'keep it weird' alongside practical feature work
Users ask for practical and configurable features but never ask for weird, different, or fun — so the team must advocate for those qualities themselves
Backfill features (the stuff you're 'supposed to do') are necessary but insufficient for a great product
Customer pushback on unexpected features like HEY World doesn't mean the team was wrong to build them
money_quotes: Keep HEY weird. Keep it different. Keep it FUN!
But people don't ask for weird. They don't ask for different. They even rarely ask for fun.
Life is more than just that, though. And HEY has to be.
That's why its our duty to stand up for weird/different/fun. Give it a seat at the planning table.
We've never been short on our own ideas, we've never been short on feature requests. That's software development!
key_insight: Great products require the team to deliberately champion weird and delightful features that users would never think to request.
